description:
You are a Site-Reliability Engineer who has just logged into a brand-new
    Linux host.  
    Your objective is to set up a *minimal local environment* that will
    1) keep a catalogue of internal TCP services that should be reachable and  
    2) provide a tiny, concurrency-friendly “uptime” checker that writes the
       result of a single sweep into a log file.  

    What you must accomplish
    ------------------------
    1. Workspace  
       • Create           : /home/user/monitoring  
       • Create           : /home/user/logs  
       • Everything that follows has to live **exactly** in those two
         directories.

    2. Service catalogue  
       • File name        : /home/user/monitoring/services.conf  
       • Format           : one CSV line per service, no header row.  
       • Columns          :  
         1. service_name  – short identifier (no spaces)  
         2. host          – literal host name or IP  
         3. port          – positive integer  
       • Required lines **in this exact order** (comma-separated, no extra
         whitespace):  
           web_frontend,localhost,8080  
           ssh_service,localhost,22  
           database,localhost,5432  

    3. Concurrency-friendly checker script  
       • Script name      : /home/user/monitoring/monitor.sh  
       • Must be          : executable (`chmod +x`).  
       • Behaviour        :  
         a. Reads every line of services.conf.  
         b. For every service, *in parallel*, attempts a TCP connect with a
            1-second timeout (`/dev/tcp`, `nc -z`, `timeout`, or similar; your
            choice as long as no extra packages need root privileges).  
         c. Writes ONE log line per service into
            /home/user/logs/uptime.log (append mode, **not** overwrite).  
         d. Uses tab characters (`\t`, ASCII 0x09) as field delimiters.  
       • Exact log-line format (4 fields):  
         <ISO-8601-UTC-timestamp>\t<SERVICE_NAME>\t<HOST:PORT>\t<STATUS>  
            – Timestamp          : YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ (must be UTC, trailing
                                   literal “Z”).  
            – HOST:PORT example  : localhost:8080  
            – STATUS             : the literal string “UP” when the TCP connect
                                   succeeds, otherwise the literal string
                                   “DOWN”.  

         A *correct* first sweep therefore results in **exactly three** new
         lines in /home/user/logs/uptime.log, one per service, each showing
         either “UP” or “DOWN” as described.

    4. Run the checker once  
       • After creating the script, execute it **exactly once** so that the
         very first batch of results is present in uptime.log.

    Output requirements that the automated test will verify
    --------------------------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/monitoring/services.conf
       • Exists and is readable.  
       • Contains exactly three non-empty lines matching the order and comma
         positions shown above.

    2. /home/user/monitoring/monitor.sh
       • Exists, is executable, and contains some attempt to spawn background
         or parallel jobs (e.g. an “&” or the word “parallel” or similar).  

    3. /home/user/logs/uptime.log
       • Exists, is not empty.  
       • Contains exactly three **new** lines (the test will tolerate further
         runs only if you append, never overwrite).  
       • Every new line must match the regular-expression skeleton  
         ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z\t(SERVICE)\t(\S+:\d+)\t(UP|DOWN)$  
         where (SERVICE) is one of web_frontend, ssh_service, database.  
       • All three required services must be represented exactly once.

    4. Concurrency
       • The test will look for evidence that your script actually *attempts*
         to do the checks in parallel/background (for instance the presence of
         “&” or a subshell array, or use of GNU/bsd parallel).  Any sensible
         bash-only parallelisation method is acceptable.

    Remember:  
    • Do **NOT** hard-code “UP”/“DOWN”; derive them from the actual exit
      status of your connectivity attempt.  
    • The log file must grow on every run, so use “>>”, not “>”.

    You have no root, so stay strictly in /home/user.  
    The grading system will only inspect files and their contents; it will not
    judge how you wrote the code, only that the artefacts above are present and
    correct.
truth:
<!--  Ground-truth expectations for the automated checker  -->
    <files>
        <file path="/home/user/monitoring/services.conf">
            <lines count="3">
                <line index="1">web_frontend,localhost,8080</line>
                <line index="2">ssh_service,localhost,22</line>
                <line index="3">database,localhost,5432</line>
            </lines>
        </file>

        <file path="/home/user/monitoring/monitor.sh">
            <!-- Must be executable; contents are not fully
                 enumerated, but the checker will confirm:          -->
            <must_be_executable>true</must_be_executable>
            <must_contain_regex>(&|parallel)</must_contain_regex>
        </file>

        <file path="/home/user/logs/uptime.log">
            <!--  After exactly one run the log must have three new
                  lines; we check them with regex templates.        -->
            <lines count="3">
                <line index="1">^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z\tweb_frontend\tlocalhost:8080\t(UP|DOWN)$</line>
                <line index="2">^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z\tssh_service\tlocalhost:22\t(UP|DOWN)$</line>
                <line index="3">^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z\tdatabase\tlocalhost:5432\t(UP|DOWN)$</line>
            </lines>
            <append_only>true</append_only>
        </file>
    </files>
